title: Module Lattice Security (Part IV): Probabilistic Polynomial Quantum Attack on Module-LWE over 2-Power Cyclotomics
abstract: We present a quantum attack on ML-KEM and related 2-power cyclotomic lattice schemes. Combining with Parts I-III, we provide an algorithm and verify the resulting approximation factor satisfies $γ\le 21 < q/2=1665$ for ML-KEM-1024, with a success probability $\ge 0.99$. We apply a tower decomposition of the Principal Ideal Problem (PIP) through the chain $\Q \subset \Q(ζ_8) \subset \cdots \subset \Q(ζ_{2^k})$ which yields a polynomial-time quantum algorithm costing $O(n^3 \log^2 n)$ gates, $O(n^2 \log n)$ qubits, and $\mathrm{poly}(n)$ classical bit operations. We extend the analysis to Falcon, Hawk, and NTRU over 2-power cyclotomic rings. This means that ML-KEM, Falcon, Hawk, NTRU-HPS, and NTRU-HRSS with all standardized parameter sets are broken under quantum attack.
summary: This paper presents a quantum algorithm that breaks several post-quantum cryptographic standards, including ML-KEM and Falcon, by solving the Principal Ideal Problem in polynomial time. It achieves this by exploiting the algebraic structure of 2-power cyclotomic rings through a tower decomposition. The results suggest that the security of these lattice-based primitives is significantly lower than previously assumed under quantum attack.
main_problem: Developing a probabilistic polynomial-time quantum attack on Module-LWE (MLWE) schemes, specifically targeting ML-KEM, Falcon, Hawk, and NTRU over 2-power cyclotomic rings.
main_result: The paper presents a quantum algorithm that reduces the Principal Ideal Problem (PIP) complexity to polynomial time, effectively breaking the standardized parameter sets of ML-KEM, Falcon, Hawk, and NTRU.
method: The attack uses an extended CDPR pipeline involving module-to-ideal reduction, a quantum tower decomposition of the Principal Ideal Problem via the Abelian Hidden Subgroup Problem, and classical log-unit CVP using Babai's algorithm.
model_or_system: The mathematical framework involves module lattices over 2-power cyclotomic rings R = Z[zeta], utilizing the algebraic structure of cyclotomic towers and the log-unit lattice.
key_observables: The approximation factor (gamma), quantum gate complexity, qubit requirements, and the CVP residual.
important_parameters: ML-KEM-1024 parameters (n=256, q=3329), ring degree (n), module rank (d), and the approximation factor threshold (q/2).
main_assumptions: The attack relies on the validity of the Hidden Subgroup Problem (HSP) solution, the use of the tower decomposition, and certain empirical/numerical results from previous parts of the series.
figures_summary: The notes mention a table summarizing quantum resource costs for ML-KEM-1024 and a table comparing theoretical vs. simulated approximation factors for Falcon.
paper_structure: The paper follows a logical progression from module-to-ideal reduction to a recursive quantum tower PIP algorithm, followed by complexity analysis, security evaluation of specific NIST standards, and a summary of cumulative improvements over previous works.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
